title: Ambition
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Ambition
summary: Scripture neither condemns ambition nor celebrates it uncritically — it redirects it, asking not 'How high can I climb?' but 'For whose glory am I climbing?'
body:
Paul was unashamedly ambitious. He made it his ambition to preach the gospel where Christ had not been named [verse:rom.15.20]. His drive was intense, his work ethic extraordinary, and his scope global. But the direction of his ambition was outward and upward — toward God's purposes, not personal empire.

Jesus addressed self-serving ambition directly when James and John requested seats at his right and left. His response inverted the world's power structure: whoever would be great among you must be your servant [verse:mark.10.43-44]. Ambition is not forbidden — but its expression in God's kingdom looks like service.

The tower of Babel stands as Scripture's cautionary tale about misplaced ambition. The builders said: let us make a name for ourselves [verse:gen.11.4]. The ambition was self-referential — greatness for its own sake — and God scattered them.

Paul draws a distinction in Philippians between selfish ambition and the mind of Christ. Do nothing from selfish ambition or conceit, but in humility count others more significant than yourselves [verse:phil.2.3]. The problem is not the drive but the direction.

Jeremiah offers a sobering word to Baruch, his scribe, who apparently had personal aspirations: do you seek great things for yourself? Do not seek them [verse:jer.45.5]. The question cuts to motive: great things for yourself, or great things for God?

Scripture does not produce passive people. It produces people whose considerable energy is aimed at something larger than themselves.

verses.annotation: Paul's ambition: to preach where no one had preached — drive aimed at God's mission.
Greatness redefined as servanthood — ambition's expression inverted.
Babel's ambition: making a name for ourselves — self-referential drive scattered.
Nothing from selfish ambition — the problem is direction, not intensity.
Seeking great things for yourself? Don't. Motive matters.
Work heartily as for the Lord — excellence directed toward God.

scholars.quote: Schreiner notes that Paul's missionary ambition in Romans 15:20 was fueled by theological conviction, not personal ego — his drive to reach unreached places flowed from his understanding of God's cosmic plan.

Sarna reads the Babel narrative as a critique of collective hubris — the builders' ambition was not wrong because it was large but because it was self-enclosed, with no reference to the God who gave them their abilities.

Fee argues that Paul's 'selfish ambition' in Philippians 2:3 translates a Greek word (eritheia) associated with political factionalism — it describes ambition that advances the self at the expense of community.
scholars.source: Paul's Letter to the Philippians (Fee, NICNT, 1995)
